story:
After precisely thirty-three grueling minutes of the most trying first date she’d ever endured, Jennifer stood. “Excuse me. I have to visit the restroom.” Bob, certain he was a gentleman, also stood and cheerfully overexplained where the ladies’ room might be—“or Women, or Gals, I never go there”—then giggled at his own joke. Jennifer rolled her eyes and left. Bob, very pleased with himself, took her crossed arms, slumped shoulders, slow head shakes, and twiddling thumbs as signs of awe. Every time he paused, she said “What?” as if she’d missed something brilliant, which he took as proof he was fascinating. While she was gone, he cued up photos of his goldfish, confident she’d love them. Yes, indeed, the date was going better than he could have hoped.

Forty minutes later, she had not returned. Bob lit a cigarette. The bartender told him there was no smoking; he asked why, muttered they should put up a sign, and flicked the cigarette to the floor. Concern finally dawning, he asked the bartender to check the ladies’ room. Empty, she reported—though the back door stood open. “She probably had an emergency,” he said, scribbling his number on a napkin. The bartender tossed it in the trash as he left without tipping, cut to the front of a long taxi line, and rode off, mystified by everyone’s middle fingers. “Sheesh. What’s their problem?”

Back at his building, he crumpled an eviction notice and found his goldfish floating belly-up. “What happened to you?” he asked, forgetting he hadn’t fed them all week despite their mouths pressed to the glass. He deleted a voicemail from his landlord—“I wonder what she wants”—found nothing from Jennifer, and decided he knew where to find her anyway.

At the clinic, the receptionist, Carine Wuthers, asked him to wait. After a long time, she returned: “Doctor Jennifer isn’t available.” Bob chuckled, then made a crude, intrusive joke about her still being in the bathroom, grinning at his supposed sensitivity. “Are you serious? Or just an idiot?” Carine asked. Bob studied her nameplate, tried small talk about Wuthering Heights, then told her to let Jennifer know he was waiting. Thirty-five minutes later he lit another cigarette; Carine’s glare returned. “Do you know this is a doctor’s office?” He offered to show her fish pictures.

Jennifer, in a white coat and holding files, rounded the corner and stopped. “Oh my god. You’re still here?” Carine said she’d tried to get him to leave. “Follow me,” Jennifer said sharply. “And put out the cigarette.” “Not here either? Sheesh,” he muttered.

In her office, Jennifer stayed standing. “No need to sit, Bob. This won’t take long.” He sat anyway. She kept her voice firm. “I’m sure you’re a decent person, and someone will be right for you. I’m not. Last night was one of the most boring half-hours of my life. When I got home, I cried.” Bob nodded as if he understood. “I have that effect on women. But why didn’t you call?” She blinked, then answered. “You’re pathetically self-centered. You only talk about yourself. I just don’t care.” He offered to pivot to his college career next time. “There will be no next time,” she said. “Please take a hint and go.”

Momentarily confused, he stood. “Okay. I’ll call tomorrow in case you change your mind.”

On the way home he was soaked in a torrential rain—no umbrella, though the forecast had been clear about it. At his door, he found his furniture in the hallway and a new lock on his apartment.
